item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I played Total War Rome 2 a lot recently, went looking for a Roman scip, found this, which was itself inspired by the same game, and oh my…
I love it I love it I love it. It's a unique idea, it's well executed, it's funny, and it fits the history well. Brilliant. Damn Carthage, they've murdered too many brave Romans!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see how this is remotely anomalous, who doesn't believe that Carthage must be destroyed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My speciality is in 20th century history, and I tend to forget the classical eras at times.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

This isn't the only piece of Roman architecture on the site that has odd effects, another being SCP-2766. It would be likely that the Foundation would look into whether the spirits they know power 2766's effects also are the cause of the effect seen in with this bridge what causes you to hate Carthage. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Stones which possess a carving relating to the Roman god Janus by an unidentified mason have been recovered from the area containing SCP-2513. SCP-2513 incorporates several of these stones into its construction, and further testing involving these stones is considered a priority to further understanding of the object.

To this:

Stones which possess a carving relating to the Roman god Janus by an unidentified mason have been recovered from the area containing SCP-2513. SCP-2513 incorporates several of these stones into its construction, and further testing involving these stones is considered a priority to further understanding of the object.1

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Similarities between SCP-2513 and the effects of SCP-2766 are still under investigation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I bet they'd commit suicide

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would like to say that this exchange made me laugh literally out loud. Good show.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … that's actual real-world history.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Brilliant idea and brilliant execution.

Also, it looks like that, in this universe, Rome and Carthage, really, really, really hated each other's guts.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a fan of ancient history, Ancient Rome in particular, I love this scip.

Upvote +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A pretty simple idea executed fantastically. The effects it has on someone who doesn't know what Carthage is are hilarious. Relatively short, straightforward, and entertaining, without being reduced to nothing but jokes.

I like that it doesn't try to be more than it is. Prolonged exposure doesn't make you think you're a centurion or give you an aneurysm, it really is just a bridge that makes you hate Carthage. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there any more anachronistic phrase than 'nuke Carthage'? LOL +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, what happens if you walk halfway across, then climb off?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely surprised they didn't test it on a Tunisian.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Laughed out loud at "thing that does a thing." Literary criticism at its finest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this article.
I would be interested in the following tests:
-Someone from Tunisia crossing south to north.
-Someone who already hates Carthage crossing north to south.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
that alone deserves a plus one.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"you still want to burn Carthage?"
"sir, i wanted to NUKE Carthage."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this.

As a minor touch, I also liked that the long-term effect was just "and then it started fading". That so rarely happens that it was actually a surprise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept doesn't particularly grab me, but the writing was fantastic. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Brilliantly hilarious. [+1]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a Classics major, this SCP makes me happy. The experiment logs are a particularly nice touch.

Quoque, Carthago delenda est.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Latin student in me couldn't help but love this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All our things are things that do things. Even I myself am a thing that does a thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: -1 I don't really like this SCP. It's a thing that does a thing. Yes, it does have a historical backstory to it. But, just walking over it and saying that a dead civilization needs to be killled is not that interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a history buff, I was amused and tickled. As an SCP fan the article was well written. Carthago delenda est.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, the dark SCPs were first, and the silly ones tend to be newer. I think it all comes down to a balance between the two.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A simple idea executed to perfection. I'd like to praise you, I feel like the site's been trending towards incredibly dark SCP's lately, but you've shown that they can still be funny and serious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I always love a good ol' roman skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1'd at "Sir, I wanted us to nuke Carthage."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I imagine it would just make them hate it more/give them brain damage. Just another reason to keep it contained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if someone who already hates Carthage north to south?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was destroyed and unusable as a bridge until 2006(after the aforementioned 1693 earthquake). The restoration made it usable again, at which point the effects became a bit of a problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I quite like this SCP - the historical element adds to it, and the effect itself is well described and well thought out.

One nitpick: The essay contains the line: "and restored in 2006 by the Ragusa Heritage Society" This indicates that the Foundation only set up containment as of a few years ago, and that it was accessible to civilians as of 2006. It seems hard to believe that that could be the case, without there being public knowledge worldwide of this effect. If that was the intention, that this bridge was publicly accessible as of 2006, this should be expanded upon. Otherwise, that line should be removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the one hand, compulsion.

On the other hand, seriously, fuck Carthage. Think they are so fucking great with their elephants, get totally scammed by Dido like a bunch of idiots.

In conclusion, Carthago delenda est.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Carthages eunt domus? People called Carthaginians they go to the house?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I think it essentially went: You talking about hating Carthage, Blarghalt making a joke about how "there should be an SCP" and me stopping whatever the hell else it was I was doing at the time and saying "Uh, no there should DEFINITELY be an SCP". A super early draft by Blarghalt was mostly an experiment log containing the line "fucking backstabbing elephant-riding son of a bitch!" and I was like "I made a good choice".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The effects don't wear off until you go back across the bridge. Since it's just a matter of a short walk, it's always carried out so there'd be no reason to blank the memories of someone that was still being affected by 2513.

And the effect is immediate. Most people on some level know the hatred has no rational basis, but only those with incredibly strong wills can resist it. Even then, not for very long.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, what happens if you make someone hate Carthage, then amnesticize them? Does that end the effect immediately, or do they still hate Carthage for no reason they can explain?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awesome, very slim and satisfying SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It prompted me to talk about some SCP that makes you hate Carthage. Then me and Cimmerian got talking in PM and the rest is history.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was this all inspired and written based on my jibbering in chat about learning to hate Carthage in Total War Rome 2?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fuck Carthage.

This actually makes a lot of sense. I mean, who needs propaganda when you can literally magic someone into hating your enemies? I feel like this is really good use of history in an SCP in that knowledge of the history isn't required to enjoy the SCP. Good job, gentlemen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Amazing how a joke idea can blossom into something wonderful. This was super fun to write.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Full credit to my co-author on this piece Blarghalt for writing the funniest lines. Image is CC and found here: http://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ilid%C5%BEa_-_Roman_bridge.jpg .

Photo was created by Pudelek.
